route.event: 29–40 passengers disembarked (operator vs. Dutch MFA discrepancy); index-case remains + widow offloaded

arrived ~10:30 AM (confirmed); 25 crew + 2 health workers disembarked in controlled phases to dockside quarantine; remains of index case removed for cremation; 23 crew in 6-week cabin quarantine aboard ship; ship disinfection completed (est. 3–4 days). As of early June 2026 the vessel was reported cleared to return to service, with Rotterdam's GGD finding no public-health obstacle.

cases.notes: Index case. Dutch national. Died aboard near Antarctic Peninsula; body preserved on board and offloaded at St. Helena on 24 Apr. No laboratory confirmation performed. WHO working hypothesis: acquired infection through environmental (rodent) exposure in Argentina before boarding.
Widow of Case 1. Disembarked at St. Helena 24 Apr, removed from KLM flight KL592 (JNB→AMS) on 25 Apr due to illness; died in South African hospital. PCR-confirmed 4 May.
British male, age 69. Medically evacuated 27 April to South Africa. PCR-confirmed 2 May. Status upgraded from critical to improving by 11 May.
Presented with pneumonia. Most recent death in the cluster (last fatality 2 May).

Developed symptoms during repatriation flight. PCR-confirmed 11 May. On life support (ECMO/artificial lung) as of 16 May; French health authorities describe as 'final stage of supportive care'. Remained on ECMO at Hôpital Bichat (Paris); no confirmed outcome reported as of 27 May. As of early June 2026, no further deterioration reported — still critical on ECMO. All 26 close contacts of the two French cases tested PCR-negative.

additional_cases_note: As of 27 May 2026 the cluster totals 13 cases — 11 confirmed and 2 probable, with 3 deaths (case fatality ratio 23%) (WHO DON604, published 28 May 2026; ECDC, case count as of 26 May 2026). The two most recent confirmed cases are a crew member who had been isolating in the Netherlands (PCR-positive 22 May, raising the total to 12) and a Spanish national already quarantining at the Gómez Ulla Military Hospital in Madrid (PCR-positive 25 May while in isolation, raising the total to 13). ECDC revised its case definition so that confirmation requires detection of Andes virus by PCR and/or serology, reclassifying the two recent cases as confirmed. No new deaths have been reported since 2 May, and no new cases since 25 May. WHO estimates the outbreak's effective reproduction number (Rt) at 0.7 as of 22 May — below the threshold for self-sustaining transmission — with a mean incubation period of 22 days and a 96% probability of safe release from quarantine at 42 days post-exposure. More than 600 contacts have now been identified across 32 countries, territories and areas (about 53% high-risk, 47% low-risk) — the first published global aggregate, and far below the 1,400–1,800 figure circulating on social media. The previously-inconclusive American case remains officially removed (negative third PCR, no antibodies detected; ECDC, 19 May 2026); the United States reports no confirmed cases, and the 18 repatriated US passengers completed 21-day monitoring at the UNMC/Nebraska biocontainment facility through 31 May (CDC had issued formal quarantine orders for 2). The MV Hondius completed disinfection at Rotterdam and, as of early June 2026, was reported cleared to return to service, with Rotterdam's municipal health service (GGD) finding no public-health obstacle. A hantavirus death in Douglas County, Colorado (18 May) was confirmed by state officials as unrelated to the Hondius cluster — Sin Nombre strain, local rodent exposure, no connection to Andes virus or cruise-ship travel. June 2026 update: As of 4 June, no new cases have been reported in 11 consecutive days (last confirmed case: 25 May). The French passenger (Case 9) remains on ECMO at Hôpital Bichat-Claude-Bernard in Paris — still critical but no further deterioration reported as of early June. Contact monitoring continues through ~6 July 2026 (42 days from the 25 May last-known exposure date). The first peer-reviewed case series was published in the New England Journal of Medicine (Andes Hantavirus Outbreak on a Cruise Ship, 2026; NEJMc2606496).

incubation_period.safe_release: WHO estimates a 96% probability of safe release from quarantine at 42 days post-exposure (DON604).
incubation_period.note: The 4–42 day range has been the CDC's documented incubation period for Andes virus since the 1990s. WHO's most recent guidance gives a mean of 22 days, a typical range of 1–6 weeks (i.e. 7–42 days), and a potential range of 1–8 weeks.

contacts_traced.summary: WHO now reports more than 600 contacts identified across 32 countries, territories and areas, of which about 53% are high-risk and 47% low-risk (WHO DON604, 28 May 2026) — the first published global aggregate. This remains far below the 1,400–1,800 figure circulating on social media, which no WHO, ECDC, or CDC document supports.
contacts_traced.st_helena_disembarked: 29 (per Oceanwide Expeditions) or ~40 (per Dutch Foreign Ministry) — number remains disputed
contacts_traced.st_helena_high_risk: small number of higher-risk contacts isolating for 45 days
contacts_traced.united_states: 18 repatriated passengers completed 21-day monitoring at the UNMC/Nebraska biocontainment facility through 31 May; CDC issued formal quarantine orders for 2. No confirmed US cases.
contacts_traced.united_kingdom: 2 passengers self-isolating plus a small number of secondary contacts
contacts_traced.south_africa: contacts from KLM flight KL592 (88 people aboard) being traced
contacts_traced.limitations: Some passengers who disembarked at St. Helena returned home before contact tracing was established. WHO acknowledges tracing is incomplete, but with the effective reproduction number (Rt) estimated at 0.7 as of 22 May and no secondary cases identified after disembarkation, wider-population risk is assessed as 'extremely low'. Andes virus typically requires close, prolonged contact for human-to-human transmission.

clarifications.claim: WHO quietly changed the incubation period to 'as early as 4 days'
clarifications.detail: The 4-day lower bound (range 4–42 days) is the long-standing CDC figure, in place since the 1990s. WHO's most recent guidance (DON604, 28 May 2026) gives a mean incubation of 22 days and a 96% probability of safe release at 42 days; its earlier DON601 gives a typical range of 1–6 weeks (minimum 1 week, not 4 days). No 'quiet change' occurred.

clarifications.claim: 1,400 to 1,800 people are unknowingly exposed and walking around
clarifications.detail: WHO's latest update (DON604, 28 May 2026) reports more than 600 contacts identified across 32 countries, territories and areas (about 53% high-risk, 47% low-risk) — the first published global aggregate, and well below 1,400. The ship itself carried 147 people. The 1,400–1,800 number appears to be a social-media estimate, not an official one, and no WHO, ECDC, or CDC document publishes such a figure.

clarifications.claim: Authorities can't confirm if 24 or 40 passengers got off the ship at St. Helena
clarifications.detail: Oceanwide Expeditions reports 29 passengers disembarked at St. Helena on 24 April. The Dutch Foreign Ministry cites approximately 40. The 24 figure is not in any source we found. The 29-vs-40 discrepancy is real and reflects incomplete manifest reconciliation, but neither end of the range is 24.

clarifications.claim: A new, invisible chain of community transmission is about to start
clarifications.detail: Andes virus is the only hantavirus with documented human-to-human transmission, but transmission typically requires close, prolonged contact (household, intensive caregiving). WHO estimates the effective reproduction number (Rt) at 0.7 as of 22 May — below the threshold for self-sustaining spread — and no secondary (post-disembarkation) cases have been identified as of late May 2026. WHO and ECDC both assess wider-population risk as 'extremely low'.

clarifications.claim: A Colorado hantavirus death is part of the Hondius outbreak
clarifications.detail: A Douglas County, Colorado adult died of hantavirus on May 18, 2026. Colorado state health officials and the CDC confirmed this case is completely unrelated to the MV Hondius cluster. The Colorado case involves Sin Nombre hantavirus (the strain endemic to North American deer mice), which does NOT spread person-to-person. The Hondius cluster is Andes virus, a separate South American strain. The Colorado patient had no known exposure to the cruise ship or its passengers.

clarifications.claim: FIFA games / mass events will accelerate spread
clarifications.detail: Andes virus is not influenza or SARS-CoV-2; it does not spread efficiently in casual crowd settings. Documented person-to-person transmission has only occurred among close household and healthcare contacts of symptomatic patients. There is no public-health basis for linking FIFA or similar mass gatherings to outbreak amplification.

secondary.market: Hantavirus lab leak confirmed by June 30?
